Sparse Attention Mechanisms: A Survey

Standard self-attention in transformer models scales quadratically with sequence length,
making it impractical for long documents. This survey examines sparse attention mechanisms
that reduce this complexity.

Window-based attention restricts each token to attend only to a local window of size w,
reducing complexity from O(n squared) to O(n times w). This is effective for tasks
where local context is sufficient.

Global attention tokens attend to all positions while local tokens attend only to nearby
positions. This hybrid approach maintains global coherence while reducing overall compute.

Learned sparsity patterns allow the model to dynamically select which positions to attend
to, potentially capturing long-range dependencies more efficiently than fixed patterns.

The key insight is that most attention weights in practice are near zero. Sparse attention
exploits this by computing only the significant interactions, yielding both speed and
memory improvements with minimal quality loss.

Applications include long document summarization, genomics, and audio processing where
sequences commonly exceed tens of thousands of tokens.
